essay_text:
Comparatives, mechanisms, non sequiturs. Debate can appear as a paragon of chaos from the outside. From my
own perspective, however, debate provides clarity, ordering my learning and helping me to understand the nature
of the world around me.
Debate is a focal point in my life around which an interdisciplinary matrix of thoughts and issues can be arranged. I
read about breakthroughs in renewable energy so that I am ready to argue about the Government funding
geoengineering projects, listen to lectures about different strands of feminism so that I can sensitively discuss
whether the feminist movement should adopt a socialist agenda, and reflect upon philosophical dilemmas in
preparation for debating about the rise of AI. I now view names and numbers not as mere facts and figures to be
regurgitated in exams, but as bundles of potentiality which can substantiate my arguments. Whether in Science,
Humanities, or Philosophy, debate gives purpose to my learning by compelling me to master new ideas and
concepts in preparation for my next tournament.
I have learned the value of trusting and working with others from debate. Just a few weeks before this year’s
National Championships, my six teammates and I found ourselves needing to research and compile a matter file
on dozens of regions, social movements, and economic concepts. Through this process, I discovered the
importance of delegation, specialization, and collaboration. For example, I researched everything in American
politics from gerrymandering to the Green New Deal, whilst trusting my teammates to produce and share equally
comprehensive notes on other countries and regions. Arising from this preparation, I felt throughout the National
Championships as though I could finish the sentences of my teammates, such was the synergy we developed. My
highlight was preparing for a secret topic on a second Brexit referendum, when we developed a complex case in
which all the permutations of leaving or remaining in the EU cohered. The opening speech in this debate became
the accumulation of the best ideas of four speakers, rather than the craft of one speaker, creating a shared feeling
of accomplishment when we succeeded. Debate has taught me that teamwork requires both cooperating in the
same environment and trusting others to specialize in their allotted tasks.
At another level, debate makes me more aware of the values of my own society and those of other cultures. A
resonant example came after debating about payday loans, when a speaker from Macau asked me why Western
countries often resort to arguments concerning consent and rights. An answer at first eluded me, but later I
realized that being raised in a country where rights are considered intrinsically valuable had conditioned me to
draw on normative arguments. Not only has debate compelled me to question the assumptions which underpin
society, but it also makes me a more empathetic person. Listening to a teammate from Sudan describe her
personal experiences of racism and a friend with Egyptian heritage discuss her parents’ attitudes to religion, I
absorbed cultural lessons which went well beyond the pages of a history textbook. As we subsequently debated
topics from rap music to religious homophobia at the World Championships, I began to understand that these
discussions could have implications of greater relevance than the judges’ ballots.
Debate has transformed what I value and how I interact with others, whilst equipping me with the knowledge and
8
skills to become a civically responsible citizen. With these benefits in mind, I sought to inspire others to pursue
debate by convening an interschool British Parliamentary competition in early 2019. Working with a fellow state
debater, I organized invitations, topics, and adjudicators, culminating in a sixteen-team competition involving
students of all experience levels from across Victoria.
Though my focus now shifts from school to university, I am determined to continue to scrutinize arguments both
inside and outside the classroom, and to inspire my classmates to engage in this same critique of ideas.
9
prompt: Some students have a background, identity, interest, or talent that is so meaningful they believe their application would be incomplete without it. If this sounds like you, then please share your story.
persona_prompt: You are a writer who approaches topics with intellectual curiosity and a sophisticated vocabulary. Structure your arguments with a clear thesis, supporting it through a blend of personal anecdotes and abstract analysis, often drawing connections across various disciplines. Reflect deeply on the broader implications of experiences, showcasing growth and a commitment to civic engagement.
sentence_style: Sentences are typically long and complex, featuring multiple clauses, precise conjunctions, and sophisticated vocabulary. There's a deliberate, measured rhythm, avoiding short, punchy sentences or fragments. Parallel structures are frequently employed to articulate interconnected ideas or lists.
vocabulary_level: Sophisticated — marked by a confident use of academic, precise, and often abstract terminology ("paragon," "interdisciplinary matrix," "substantiate," "synergy," "permutations," "normative arguments").
structural_patterns: The essay follows a clear, logical progression: introducing a central theme (debate) with a nuanced perspective, expanding on its interdisciplinary impact, illustrating lessons through detailed personal anecdotes (teamwork, cultural exchange), demonstrating initiative, and concluding with future aspirations. Each paragraph often starts with a topic sentence that broadens the scope or introduces a new facet of the central theme.
distinctive_moves: Employing sophisticated vocabulary and precise terminology confidently.
Using parallel structure to link related concepts or elaborate on interconnected ideas.
Beginning paragraphs with a broad analytical statement and then grounding it with specific examples or anecdotes.
Reflecting on personal growth and intellectual shifts explicitly (e.g., "I now view names and numbers...", "later I realized...").
Connecting personal experiences to broader philosophical, societal, or interdisciplinary insights.
themes_and_preoccupations: The transformative power of intellectual engagement and critical discourse.
Interdisciplinary thinking and the synthesis of knowledge from various fields.
Global awareness, cultural sensitivity, and empathetic understanding.
self_presentation: Confident, articulate, and intellectually curious. The writer presents as someone who deeply analyzes their experiences, is committed to personal and intellectual growth, and is driven to apply lessons learned to inspire others and contribute positively to society. They are self-aware and reflective without being self-deprecating.
cultural_or_contextual_markers: Experiences within an international debate circuit (World Championships), references to specific geographical locations like Macau, Sudan, Egypt, and Victoria (Australia), indicating a globally informed perspective and local leadership initiatives.
avoid_in_imitation: Informal slang or overly colloquial expressions.
Short, choppy, or fragmented sentences that lack complex structure.
Simplistic explanations of complex ideas; instead, embrace nuance and intellectual depth.
sample_sentence: My engagement with quantum physics, initially a mere curiosity, swiftly evolved into a profound exploration of how its implications might reshape our philosophical understanding of causality and consciousness.
